text.title: PHP Laravel: Eloquent Model Patterns
text.summary: Eloquent model definition patterns: fillable, casts, relationships, scopes, accessors, and the repository pattern for data access.

rule.statement: Always define $fillable explicitly — never use $guarded = []. Define $casts for all non-string attributes (datetime, boolean, array/JSON). Hide $hidden fields (password, remember_token) at the model level.
rule.rationale: $guarded = [] allows any column to be mass-assigned. Explicit $fillable and $casts are the first defense against property injection and datetime format inconsistencies.

rule.statement: Define Eloquent scopes for reusable query constraints (scopeActive, scopeSearch, scopeWithRole). Call scopes from the repository, not from controllers or services directly.
rule.rationale: Scopes encapsulate query logic with the model. Repeating WHERE clauses across service methods produces inconsistent behavior when the business rule changes.

example.note: Accessor/mutator for email: use Attribute::make(set: fn($v) => strtolower($v)) to normalize on write, not on read.

rule.statement: Add a Repository class only when there is a real second backing implementation (e.g., Postgres primary + ElasticSearch search). A UserRepository that delegates all calls to User::query() adds indirection with no value — Eloquent is already a repository.
rule.rationale: An unnecessary repository layer means two places to change for every schema update: the model and the repository. Agents emit it by default from older tutorials.

rule.statement: When a repository exists, eager-load all relations needed by the caller: $this->model->with(['roles', 'orders'])->findOrFail($id). Never rely on lazy loading from inside a Resource.
rule.rationale: Resources that lazy-load relations produce N+1 queries on list endpoints. The query count scales with page size.

text.reference: User model with fillable, casts, relationships, scopes
UserRepository with eager loading and pagination
